artifacts.content:
Hannah,

Sitting here with my second cup of coffee before the kids wake up and thought I’d actually answer your last text like a grown-up instead of with emojis.

Farm-wise, we’re in that weird in-between where strawberries are limping along after all the rain and I’m already thinking about pumpkins. You’d laugh if you saw my spreadsheets. We’ve been pushing the pumpkin patch marketing harder the last couple of years because, honestly, a decent chunk of those October weekends – probably six to eight hundred dollars each Saturday/Sunday – goes straight to covering Dad Jenkins’s PT bills. Ever since his mild stroke at Lexington Medical Center back in spring 2016, those appointments in West Columbia have kept him moving well enough to stay in his little house on the farm, but they aren’t cheap.

On top of that, we still have that IRS payment plan hanging over us from when we fell behind on payroll taxes during the April 2020 shutdown. Remember those weeks we were closed and still trying to pay the college kids? We worked out a deal, but it means I have to peel off about $1,200 a month from CSA and stand sales and send it straight to the IRS. It’s manageable, just tight, and it makes me obsess over every flat of berries.

Kids are good. Evie’s soccer cleats are permanently muddy, and Sam is determined to learn “Smoke on the Water” at guitar even though his hands are tiny. Mom and Dad came by Sunday; Dad poked around the irrigation lines like he still works at Porter’s Hardware.

How’s baby Collins? Send me more pictures, please. I’m trying to figure out a weekend after school’s out to come up to Greenville.

Love,
Laura

Brain dump – fall season planning

1. Remind myself WHY we’re doing this: Sweetwater Creek Berry & Pumpkin Farm really has become this little family agritourism thing – U-pick berries, pumpkin patch, school field trips – that keeps Charlie here on the land and helps pay for his therapy since the 2016 stroke. The fall pumpkin patch weekends basically underwrite those PT trips to West Columbia.

2. Reality check on cash flow: I’ve got to keep that IRS payment plan in mind every time I get dreamy about adding new attractions. Since 2020–2021, when we got behind on payroll taxes during the first COVID shutdown in April 2020, that $1,200/month we send to the IRS from CSA + stand income is non‑negotiable. Build it into the mental budget like fertilizer.

3. My capacity: We’re open Thursday–Sunday for a reason. Between the bookkeeping I still do for Porter’s Hardware in Irmo and my weekly therapy with Dr. Melissa Carter at Lexington Behavioral Health (started January 2023 after the GAD diagnosis), I cannot run a seven‑day circus. No big events on Tuesday evenings – that’s therapy night, and I need it.

4. Event ideas: “Sunset in the Pumpkin Patch” series – schedule around Dr. Carter appointments. Maybe 3–4 Fridays in October, small groups, low key.

5. Longer term: Look into hiring a Clemson ag student to help with social media and field trips so I’m not trying to be in three places at once.

Breathe. One season at a time.

Hi Mrs. Watkins,

Thank you again for bringing your class out to Sweetwater Creek Berry & Pumpkin Farm this spring. Your kiddos were so sweet with the goats, and I loved hearing them try to pronounce “Camarosa.”

I also wanted to follow up on the conversation we had with the school counselor after Evie’s last meeting. Her ADHD diagnosis at Lexington Pediatric Associates back in September 2022 really opened my eyes to how overwhelming some field trip schedules can be. Based on your and the counselor’s feedback, we’ve made a few changes to our standard visit:

– We’re slowing the pace between the wagon ride, berry picking, and animal time so kids who need a little extra processing time don’t feel rushed.
– We’ve shortened the corn maze route we use with school groups and spaced the trivia questions farther apart so it’s easier for kids who struggle with attention to stay engaged without getting frustrated.
– We’re building in a short “quiet snack” break under the shade trees where teachers can regroup their classes.

We’re trying to keep the farm a low‑pressure, sensory‑friendly place for all kids, not just Evie, so I appreciate your honesty. Right now, we’re still pretty small and don’t have a whole menu of structured educational programs – it’s more of a simple wagon tour and hands‑on stations than a polished museum experience – but we’re learning as we go.

If you or the counselor have any other suggestions, I’d truly welcome them. We want your students to leave feeling successful and proud of what they learned.

Warmly,
Laura Jenkins
Sweetwater Creek Berry & Pumpkin Farm
2147 Sweetwater Creek Road, Lexington, SC 29073
info@sweetwatercreekfarm.com

metadata.subject: Tweaks to our field trip schedule

artifacts.content:
Event: Weekly therapy – Dr. Melissa Carter

Date: Tuesday, May 13, 2025
Time: 5:15–6:10 p.m.
Location: Lexington Behavioral Health, 2nd floor, Suite 204
Attendees: Laura Jenkins, Dr. Carter

Notes:
– Leave farm by 4:30 p.m. so I’m not sprinting in sweaty overalls.
– Ask Dr. Carter about this constant knot in my stomach whenever I look at the farm bank account. Between Charlie’s PT bills (stroke at Lexington Medical Center in spring 2016, still going to West Columbia for therapy) and the IRS payment plan from the April 2020 payroll tax mess, I feel like every flat of berries and pumpkin ticket is already spent.
– Talk about boundaries: we’re open Thursday–Sunday for a reason, but I keep feeling guilty when people ask why we’re not open more days. Remind myself I’m also doing Porter’s Hardware bookkeeping and can’t work 7 days without my anxiety spiraling.
– Check in about Evie’s ADHD and how to help her with transitions during soccer + homework on busy farm days.
– No “Sunset in the Pumpkin Patch” events on Tuesdays this fall – protect this slot.

To‑do before appointment:
– Print updated medication list (if any changes).
– Let Mark know he’s on kid pickup duty from Lexington Elementary.
– Bring a notebook; last time I forgot half of what we talked about by the time I got home.

Hey Mark,

I know we’ll talk about this over dinner, but I wanted to get my thoughts down while the kids are at soccer/guitar and it’s quiet in the store.

I’ve been looking at the October calendar. If we’re serious about keeping your mom in the rotation at the farm store, we need to be intentional. Since her early-stage Parkinson’s diagnosis at Prisma Health Neurology in Columbia last November, I can really see how much standing all day wipes her out. What if we keep her on 4‑hour shifts during the crazy pumpkin Saturdays – say 10–2 – and then have one of the college kids or your dad tag in for the afternoon rush? She still loves talking people through the jams and pickles, and honestly, customers adore her, but I don’t want to push her.

Same with your dad: PT in West Columbia is helping, but I noticed he was dragging after we kept him on hayride duty too long last year. Maybe shorter blocks and more breaks on the porch.

We also need to keep in mind our own limits. We’re still pretty small and basically only open to the public a handful of days outside strawberry and pumpkin season because we just don’t have the staff or my bandwidth to handle big crowds every weekend. Until we can hire that reliable seasonal person and maybe a Clemson student, I’d rather have fewer, well‑run days than total chaos.

Let’s look at the staffing sheet tonight after the kids go to bed.

Love you,
L

Parking + flow ideas – summer/fall 2025

Jotting this down between customers so I don’t forget.

Right now, parking is basically along the gravel driveway and then we wave people into the pasture when it’s dry. On busy strawberry Saturdays it feels like I’m directing traffic at the fair, and parents get nervous about kids darting between trucks. Long term, we need a more defined lot with clear rows and maybe someone stationed out there.

Complicating factors:
– Budget: with $1,200/month still going to the IRS from CSA/stand sales because of that April 2020 payroll tax fiasco, plus Charlie’s ongoing PT after his 2016 stroke, big gravel projects feel scary.
– Land use: don’t want to tear up too much pasture we might need for rotations.

Short‑term ideas:
– Ask Dad if he can come out from Irmo one or two peak weekends to help flag cars (he still loves playing “foreman,” and it gets him away from Porter’s Hardware).
– Better signage at the road: “Parking this way” arrows, maybe a simple map on the website.
– Cones to create a one‑way loop so people aren’t backing into each other.

Longer‑term dream:
– Level and gravel a proper lot near the barn.
– Add a small covered drop‑off zone for school buses during field trips so kids aren’t unloading right in the middle of everything.

Action items before fall:
– Price gravel and marking paint at Lowe’s.
– Talk with Mark about whether we can squeeze a small parking upgrade into this year’s budget or need to wait.
– Check county rules about signage on Sweetwater Creek Road.

Hi Karen,

I’ve been meaning to write since you asked how we “do it all” out here. Short answer: we don’t, but we’re trying.

Day to day, Sweetwater Creek Berry & Pumpkin Farm is still very much a family outfit – Mark, me, the kids underfoot, and his parents helping as they can. We’ve shifted more toward U-pick berries, pumpkins, and school trips since we took over after Charlie’s stroke at Lexington Medical Center in March 2016. Those fall pumpkin patch weekends are a big deal now; part of that income helps cover his ongoing physical therapy in West Columbia so he can stay in his little house on the farm.

What I didn’t really talk about at the market is how much we’ve had to build the business around my own limits. I still do part‑time bookkeeping from home for Porter’s Hardware in Irmo, and after being diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder in January 2023, I started weekly sessions with Dr. Melissa Carter at Lexington Behavioral Health. That’s why we’re only open Thursday through Sunday most weeks. I’ve learned the hard way that if I try to run the farm seven days plus kids’ activities, my anxiety spikes and I end up awake at 3 a.m. doing worst‑case‑scenario math about fuel prices and hurricanes.

We’re also quietly chipping away at an IRS payment plan from when we fell behind on payroll taxes during the April 2020 shutdown. About $1,200 a month from CSA and stand sales goes straight to that. Not glamorous, but it keeps us above water.

All that said, I still love watching a preschooler pick their first berry. That part makes the spreadsheets and therapy appointments worth it.

If you ever want to compare notes or commiserate, my porch is always open.

Take care,
Laura

metadata.subject: Re: How are you juggling everything?
